items.question: Which groupby result keeps the same number of rows as the input?
items.explanation: transform broadcasts each group's statistic back to every row in that group, preserving input length.

items.question: You must keep all event rows even when product metadata is missing. Which merge?
options: how='left' with events on the left
items.answer: how='left' with events on the left
items.explanation: Left merge preserves every left row; unmatched product keys produce NaN metadata instead of dropping events.

items.question: Which workflow causes join leakage from groupby?
options: Split, groupby on train, map to test
groupby transform on full data, then train_test_split
left merge then fillna with train median
items.answer: groupby transform on full data, then train_test_split
items.explanation: Global groupby before split lets test rows influence group statistics assigned as features.

items.question: pd.merge(left, right, on='id', how='inner') with ids {1,2,3} left and {2,3,4} right yields how many rows?
items.explanation: Inner join keeps only matching ids 2 and 3—one row per match unless keys duplicate.

items.question: What does merge(..., validate='m:1') enforce?
options: Many rows on left, many on right
Left keys may repeat; right keys must be unique
items.answer: Left keys may repeat; right keys must be unique
items.explanation: m:1 allows many left rows per key but at most one right row per key—typical fact-to-dimension joins.
